pattern: Every character produced by repeatedly appending a shifted copy of the whole word turns out to depend only on how many 1-bits are in that position's index -- the string is a letter-valued analogue of the classic doubling/Thue-Morse construction.
hints: Notice the string only ever doubles in length: after t rounds it has 2^t characters, and the second half is just the first half with every letter advanced by one.

Think recursively about position k (1-indexed): if k falls in the first half of the current doubled string, its value is the same as looking it up one round earlier; if it falls in the second half, it's one round earlier's answer shifted by one letter.
Unwinding that recursion is the same as counting how many 1-bits appear in the binary representation of (k - 1); the answer is 'a' advanced by that many letters.

edge_inputs.description: k exactly a power of two
k one past a power of two, a common off-by-one spot
k with many set bits, tests the popcount reasoning

stress.description: sweep across the whole allowed range of k
